analogy: Think of "Citizenship" through the chapter's own opening images: CitizenshipCitizenshipPolitical Theory 79 Chapter 6 Citizenship Citizenship implies full and equal membership of a political community. In this chapter we will explore what exactly this means today. In Sections 6.2 and 6.3 we will look at some debates and struggles which are going on regarding... The easy idea is that the visible surface is only the outside cover. The chapter keeps asking how we can know what is inside when no one can directly reach the deep interior. So the learner must read clues: rocks, mines, drilling, volcanic material, heat, gravity, magnetism and earthquake waves. Like solving a hidden-layer puzzle, each clue tells something about the crust, mantle, core and volcanic activity without inventing anything beyond the textbook.
full_notes:
Citizenship is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins by asking how the nature of the earth should be imagined and why the interior cannot be known in the same direct way as the surface. It connects the hidden interior with visible results on the surface, especially landscape development, earthquakes, tsunamis and volcanic activity.

The text explains that citizenshipCitizenshipPolitical Theory 79 Chapter 6 Citizenship Citizenship implies full and equal membership of a political community.

The text explains that in this chapter we will explore what exactly this means today.

The text explains that in Sections 6.2 and 6.3 we will look at some debates and struggles which are going on regarding the interpretation of the term ‘full and equal membership’.

The text explains that section 6.4 will discuss the relationship between citizens and the nation and the criteria of citizenship adopted in different countries.

The text explains that theories of democratic citizenship claim that citizenship should be universal.

The text explains that does this mean that every person today should be accepted as a member of one or other state? then How can we explain the existence of so many stateless people?

The text explains that the last section 6.6 will discuss the issue of global citizenship.

The text explains that does it exist and could it replace national citizenship?

The text explains that after going through this chapter you should be able to o explain the meaning of citizenship, and o discuss some of the areas in which that meaning is being expanded or challenged today.

The text explains that citizenshipCitizenship 80 Political Theory 6.1 INTRODUCTION Citizenship has been defined as full and equal membership of a political community.

The text explains that in the contemporary world, states provide a collective political identity to their members as well as certain rights.

The text explains that therefore we think of ourselves as Indians, or Japanese, or Germans, depending on the state to which we belong.

The text explains that citizens expect certain rights from their state as well as help and protection wherever they may travel.

The text explains that the importance of full membership of a state can be appreciated if we think of the condition of the thousands of people in the world who have the bad fortune to be forced to live as refugees or illegal migrants because no state is willing to grant them membership.

The text explains that such people are not guaranteed rights by any state and generally live in precarious conditions.

The text explains that for them full membership of a state of their choice is a goal for which they are willing to struggle, as we see today with Palestinian refugees in the Middle East.

The text explains that citizens are also considered to be the inheritors and trustees of the culture and natural resources of the country.

The text explains that if jobs, facilities like medical care or education, and natural resources like land or water, are limited, demands may be made to restrict entry to ‘outsiders’ even though they may be fellow citizens.

The text explains that the white community, instead of allowing the black people to enter some community parks as was directed by the court, decided to close them.

The text explains that they may be blamed for straining the resources of the city or for spreading crime and disease.

The text explains that these people are dependent on access to forests and other natural resources to maintain their way of life.

The text explains that many of them face threats to their way of life and livelihood because of the pressure of increasing populations and the search for land and resources to maintain them.

The text explains that pressures from commercial interests wanting to mine the resources which may exist in forests or coasts poses another threat to the way of life and livelihood of forest dwellers and tribal peoples, as does the tourist industry.

The text explains that what kind of obligation will they have to their state and fellow citizens?

The text explains that all citizens may be granted equal rights but all may not be able to equally exercise them.

The text explains that write a short note on any two struggles for full enjoyment of citizen rights which have taken place in India in recent years.

The text explains that which rights were being claimed in each case?

The text explains that what are some of the problems faced by refugees?

The text explains that in what ways could the concept of global citizenship benefit them?

The text explains that migration of people to different regions within the country is often resisted by the local inhabitants.

The text explains that what are some of the contributions that the migrants could make to the local economy?

The text explains that 6. “Democratic citizenship is a project rather than an accomplished fact even in countries like India which grant equal citizenship”.

The text explains that discuss some of the issues regarding citizenship being raised in India today.

Source: NCERT Class 11 Political Theory, Chapter 6: Citizenship

prelims_traps: Do not treat 6.1 INTRODUCTION and LET’S THINK as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Citizenship; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Citizenship through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use 6.1 INTRODUCTION and LET’S THINK to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
